section.title: Jesus by the Sea and the Restoration of Peter
section.sectionRationale: The Gospel's epilogue. By the Sea of Galilee the risen Jesus meets seven disciples who have fished all night in vain, fills their nets at His word, and shares a breakfast He has prepared. Then, beside a charcoal fire like the one where Peter denied Him, Jesus gently restores him with a threefold question, love and commission answering the threefold denial, calls him to shepherd the flock and to follow even to death, and quietly corrects the disciples' curiosity about the future.
section.thesis: The risen Jesus provides for and restores His failing disciples, filling the empty nets and, beside a charcoal fire, drawing from Peter a threefold confession of love that answers his threefold denial, recommissioning him to feed the flock and to follow to the death by which he will glorify God, so that the Gospel ends in grace, mission, and the call, follow Me.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the miraculous catch and the breakfast Jesus provides; the threefold restoration of Peter and his commission to feed the sheep; the foretelling of Peter's death and the call to follow; and the word about the beloved disciple and the Gospel's closing testimony.

verses.text: Later, by the Sea of Tiberias, Jesus again revealed Himself to the disciples. He made Himself known in this way:
Simon Peter, Thomas called Didymus, Nathanael from Cana in Galilee, the sons of Zebedee, and two other disciples were together.
Simon Peter told them, “I am going fishing.” “We will go with you,” they said. So they went out and got into the boat, but caught nothing that night.
Early in the morning, Jesus stood on the shore, but the disciples did not recognize that it was Jesus.
So He called out to them, “Children, do you have any fish?” “No,” they answered.
He told them, “Cast the net on the right side of the boat, and you will find some.” So they cast it there, and they were unable to haul it in because of the great number of fish.
Then the disciple whom Jesus loved said to Peter, “It is the Lord!” As soon as Simon Peter heard that it was the Lord, he put on his outer garment (for he had removed it) and jumped into the sea.
The other disciples came ashore in the boat. They dragged in the net full of fish, for they were not far from land, only about a hundred yards.
When they landed, they saw a charcoal fire there with fish on it, and some bread.
Jesus told them, “Bring some of the fish you have just caught.”
So Simon Peter went aboard and dragged the net ashore. It was full of large fish, 153, but even with so many, the net was not torn.
“Come, have breakfast,” Jesus said to them. None of the disciples dared to ask Him, “Who are You?” They knew it was the Lord.
Jesus came and took the bread and gave it to them, and He did the same with the fish.
This was now the third time that Jesus appeared to the disciples after He was raised from the dead.

When they had finished eating, Jesus asked Simon Peter, “Simon son of John, do you {{agapao|love}} Me more than these?” “Yes, Lord,” he answered, “You know I {{phileo|love}} You.” Jesus replied, “Feed My lambs.”
Jesus asked a second time, “Simon son of John, do you love Me?” “Yes, Lord,” he answered, “You know I love You.” Jesus told him, “Shepherd My sheep.”

Jesus asked a third time, “Simon son of John, do you love Me?” Peter was deeply hurt that Jesus had asked him a third time, “Do you love Me?” “Lord, You know all things,” he replied. “You know I love You.” Jesus said to him, “Feed My sheep.

Truly, truly, I tell you, when you were young, you dressed yourself and walked where you wanted; but when you are old, you will stretch out your hands, and someone else will dress you and lead you where you do not want to go.”
Jesus said this to indicate the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God. And after He had said this, He told him, “{{akoloutheo|Follow Me}}.”
Peter turned and saw the disciple whom Jesus loved following them. He was the one who had leaned back against Jesus at the supper to ask, “Lord, who is going to betray You?”
When Peter saw him, he asked, “Lord, what about him?”
Jesus answered, “If I want him to remain until I return, what is that to you? You follow Me!”

Because of this, the rumor spread among the brothers that this disciple would not die. However, Jesus did not say that he would not die, but only, “If I want him to remain until I return, what is that to you?”
This is the disciple who testifies to these things and who has written them down. And we know that his testimony is true.
There are many more things that Jesus did. If all of them were written down, I suppose that not even the world itself would have space for the books that would be written.

agapao.gloss: To love with self-giving, committed love. In His first two questions Jesus uses agapao, do you love Me, and Peter answers with phileo. Interpreters debate whether the shift between the two verbs is significant or simply stylistic variation, common in John. Either way, the threefold question of love graciously answers Peter's threefold denial and restores him.

phileo.gloss: To love with warm affection, the love of a friend. Peter answers each time with phileo, appealing to Jesus' knowledge of his heart, You know that I love You. Whether John intends a real distinction from agapao or a natural interchange of synonyms, Peter's insistence rests not on his own boasting but on the Lord who knows all things and knows his love.

akoloutheo.gloss: To follow, to accompany as a disciple. The Gospel that began with the call to follow ends with it: after restoring Peter and foretelling his death, Jesus simply says, follow Me. To follow is the essence of discipleship, a lifelong walking after Christ that will lead Peter even to a cross of his own, by which he will glorify God.

basic.context: The risen Jesus appears a third time to His disciples, by the Sea of Galilee. Peter and six others fish all night and catch nothing, until a figure on the shore tells them to cast on the other side, and the net fills. John recognizes Him: it is the Lord. On the shore Jesus has already made a charcoal fire and breakfast. After they eat, Jesus takes Peter aside and asks three times, do you love Me, matching Peter's three denials, and three times commissions him to feed His sheep. He foretells that Peter will one day die for Him and calls him, follow Me. When Peter asks about John, Jesus tells him to mind his own calling. The Gospel ends by affirming the truth of this eyewitness testimony.
basic.hermeneutics: This epilogue is about grace and recommissioning. The charcoal fire deliberately recalls the fire where Peter denied Jesus, and the threefold question answers the threefold denial. The key questions are, how does Jesus restore a failed disciple, and what does He call him to. He restores Peter not by ignoring the failure but by drawing out love and entrusting him with the flock. The debate over agapao and phileo should not overshadow the clear message: Peter is fully restored and commissioned, and the call is simply, follow Me.
basic.originalLanguages: The restoration turns on the verbs for love. Jesus asks with agapao, and Peter answers with phileo; the interplay, whether a real distinction or stylistic variation, frames a threefold confession of love. And the Gospel closes on the word it began with, akoloutheo, follow Me.
basic.typology: The miraculous catch of fish echoes the earlier call of the disciples to be fishers of people, now renewed for the mission ahead. The shepherd commission fulfills the pattern of Jesus the Good Shepherd entrusting under-shepherds with His flock. The charcoal fire answers the fire of denial with restoration.
basic.christ: Christ is the risen Lord who provides for His own, restores the fallen, and commissions them for service. He is the Good Shepherd who entrusts His sheep to Peter and calls him to follow even to death. He knows all things, including the love of a disciple who once failed.
basic.god: God is glorified in the faithful death of His servants, as Jesus says of Peter. The whole scene reveals a God who does not discard failures but restores and re-employs them, weaving even their falls into a deeper humility and love.
basic.crossReferences: Luke 5 tells the first miraculous catch and call. John 10 presents the Good Shepherd. John 18 recounts Peter's denial by a charcoal fire. 1 Peter 5 shows Peter later shepherding God's flock as he was commissioned.
basic.oneStory: The Gospel ends not with Peter's failure but with grace: the risen Christ restores the denier, entrusts him with the flock, and renews the call to follow. The story of Jesus flows on into the mission of His people, who feed His sheep and follow Him to the end, until He returns.

context.literary: The epilogue of John, balancing the prologue and rounding off the Gospel after the purpose statement of chapter 20. It joins a resurrection appearance and miraculous catch to the restoration of Peter and a word about the beloved disciple, closing with a note on the authorship and truthfulness of the testimony and a final flourish about the inexhaustible works of Jesus. The charcoal fire and threefold questioning form deliberate echoes of Peter's denial.
context.historical: The Sea of Tiberias is the Sea of Galilee, the disciples' home region, to which they returned after Passover. Night was the usual time for fishing. The precise count of 153 fish reflects an eyewitness's memory, though its symbolism has been much discussed. The prophecy of stretching out the hands is widely understood as a reference to Peter's later crucifixion, by tradition in Rome. The we of verse 24 reflects the community attesting the beloved disciple's testimony.
context.canonical: The catch of fish recalls the call in Luke 5:1-11 and the commission to fish for people. Peter's restoration and commission to shepherd anticipate his role in Acts and his own charge to elders in 1 Peter 5:1-4. The foretelling of Peter's death glorifying God connects to the New Testament theme of martyrdom as witness. The closing hyperbole about the world not containing the books underlines that the written Gospel is a true but selective testimony to the boundless Christ, complementing John 20:30-31.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the risen Christ restoring and recommissioning His failed disciple Peter, entrusting the flock to him and calling him to follow even to death, and to affirm the eyewitness truthfulness of the Gospel, so that readers see grace triumph over failure and hear the call to follow.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a particular appearance and Peter's unique restoration and commission. Its abiding truths are that Christ restores repentant failures, that love for Him is the heart of service, that His under-shepherds are to feed His flock, and that every disciple is called to follow Him regardless of others' paths.

debates.question: Is the shift between agapao and phileo in the questions significant?

views.label: A meaningful distinction
views.case: Jesus first asks with the higher word agapao and Peter answers with the warmer phileo; on the third question Jesus descends to Peter's word, gently meeting the humbled disciple where he is, which is why Peter is grieved.

views.label: Stylistic variation
views.case: John frequently interchanges near-synonyms, and other pairs in the passage also vary (feed and shepherd, lambs and sheep, know), so the shift may be literary variety rather than a doctrinal distinction.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage's message is clear and unshaken: Peter is fully restored through a threefold confession of love that answers his threefold denial, and is entrusted with the care of Christ's flock.

debates.question: What is the point of Jesus' answer about the beloved disciple, if I want him to remain?

views.label: A rebuke of comparison
views.case: Peter is not to measure his calling against another's; Jesus' repeated you follow Me redirects him from curiosity about John's future to his own faithfulness.

views.label: A correction of a false rumor
views.case: The narrator notes that a rumor arose that this disciple would not die, and carefully corrects it; Jesus did not promise that but only posed a hypothetical, guarding against misreading His words.

debates.bearing: Both stand together: Jesus refuses to let Peter compare callings and calls him simply to follow, and the narrator corrects the rumor, teaching readers not to build doctrine on misunderstood sayings.

study.originalLanguages: The restoration turns on the verbs for love. Jesus asks the first two times with agapao, do you love Me, and Peter each time answers with phileo, you know that I love You, appealing to the Lord who knows his heart. Whether the change of words marks a real distinction, the committed love of agapao and the warm affection of phileo, or is simply the stylistic variation common in John, the shape is unmistakable: a threefold question of love graciously undoing Peter's threefold denial, each answer met with a commission to feed and shepherd Christ's flock. And the Gospel closes on the very word with which discipleship began, akoloutheo, follow Me, the lifelong call that will lead Peter to a cross of his own by which he will glorify God.

typology.type: The miraculous catch and the call to fish for people
typology.fulfillment: The renewed commission of the disciples for the mission ahead, echoing Luke 5:1-11 and John 21:6.

typology.type: The Good Shepherd entrusting the flock
typology.fulfillment: Christ appointing Peter as an under-shepherd to feed and tend His sheep, John 10 and 21:15-17.
The charcoal fire of restoration, where the threefold question answers the threefold denial, John 18:18 and 21:9.

study.christ: Christ is the risen Lord who seeks out His disheartened disciples, provides for them abundantly, and prepares a meal with His own hands. He is the Good Shepherd who does not cast off the shepherd who failed Him but restores Peter fully, drawing out his love and entrusting to him the care of the flock. He knows all things, including the genuine love hidden beneath Peter's shame. He foretells the death by which Peter will glorify God and issues, once more, the call that has echoed from the first chapter: follow Me. The Gospel ends with the boundless Christ whose works could fill the world with books.
study.god: God is glorified in the faithful witness of His servants, even to death, as Jesus says of Peter's future. The scene reveals a God whose grace does not discard those who fail but restores and re-employs them, turning a denier into a shepherd and a martyr. The Father's purpose, carried through the Son, now flows out into the mission of the church, as restored disciples feed Christ's sheep and follow Him. The God who has been made known throughout this Gospel is shown at the last to be a God of patient, restoring grace who calls the humbled to a renewed and costly discipleship.

crossReferences.note: The earlier miraculous catch and the call to fish for people, echoed in this renewed commission.
Peter's threefold denial by a charcoal fire, answered by the threefold restoration at another charcoal fire.
Peter later charges elders to shepherd God's flock, living out the commission he received here.
Follow Me, the call with which discipleship began, now renewed at the Gospel's close.

study.oneStory: The Gospel ends not with Peter's failure but with grace. By the same sea where he was first called, and beside a charcoal fire like the one where he denied his Lord, the risen Christ restores the fallen disciple, draws from him a threefold confession of love, and entrusts him with the flock. He foretells the cross by which Peter will one day glorify God and renews the ancient call, follow Me. So the story of Jesus flows on into the mission of His people, who feed His sheep and follow Him to the end, testifying to a Savior so great that the world itself could not contain the books of all He did, until He comes again.

quiz.q: How do the disciples come to recognize Jesus on the shore?

options.text: After the miraculous catch of fish at His word, the beloved disciple says, it is the Lord.
options.note: Verses 6 and 7 show recognition dawning through the great catch.

options.text: They recognize Him at once by His appearance on the shore.
options.note: At first they did not recognize Him; it was the miraculous catch that revealed the Lord.

options.text: He announces His own name to them.
options.note: He does not name Himself; the sign of the catch leads to recognition.

options.text: They realize it is Jesus once the nets are filled.
options.note: True, but it omits that the beloved disciple names Him, it is the Lord.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus ask Peter three times, do you love Me?

options.text: To answer Peter's threefold denial with a threefold confession and restoration.
options.note: The three questions, beside a charcoal fire, deliberately match Peter's three denials.

options.text: Because Jesus was unsure of Peter's answer.
options.note: Jesus knows all things; the repetition is for Peter's restoration, not Jesus' information.

options.text: To test whether Peter was worthy to lead.
options.note: The threefold question is gracious restoration answering his threefold denial, not a test of worthiness or an act of shaming.

options.text: To draw out Peter's love and recommission him.
options.note: True, but it omits the pointed correspondence to his threefold denial.

quiz.q: What does Jesus commission Peter to do?

options.text: To feed and shepherd His lambs and sheep.
options.note: Each of the three exchanges ends with a charge to feed or shepherd the flock.

options.text: To lead the other disciples as their head.
options.note: Peter is given real leadership, but it is shepherding and feeding the flock in service, not lording it over others.

options.text: To return to fishing for a living.
options.note: The commission is to care for Christ's people, not to resume ordinary fishing.

options.text: To care for Christ's people.
options.note: True, but it does not capture the specific shepherding language, feed and tend the sheep.

quiz.q: How does Jesus respond when Peter asks about the beloved disciple's future?

options.text: He redirects Peter to his own calling, saying, what is that to you? You follow Me.
options.note: Verse 22 turns Peter from comparison back to his own discipleship.

options.text: He promises that the beloved disciple will never die.
options.note: This is the very rumor John corrects; Jesus only posed a hypothetical and redirected Peter to follow Him.

options.text: He rebukes the beloved disciple sharply.
options.note: The word is to Peter about minding his own calling, not a rebuke of John.

options.text: He tells Peter not to worry about others' paths.
options.note: True in effect, but it omits the direct command, you follow Me.

quiz.q: How does the Gospel of John conclude?

options.text: By affirming the beloved disciple's true testimony and noting that Jesus did far more than could ever be written.
options.note: Verses 24 and 25 attest the testimony and marvel at the boundless works of Jesus.

options.text: By claiming to record every deed Jesus ever did.
options.note: The opposite; it says the world could not contain the books of all He did.

options.text: By simply ending the story after a resurrection appearance.
options.note: It does end with a resurrection appearance, but it also affirms the testimony is true and marvels at Jesus' boundless works.

options.text: By pointing to the reliability of the eyewitness account.
options.note: True, but it omits the closing wonder at the inexhaustible works of Jesus.

questions: The disciples toiled all night and caught nothing until Jesus spoke. Where are you laboring in your own strength, and what would it mean to work at His word instead?
Jesus restored Peter beside a fire like the one where he failed. How does it comfort you that Christ meets us at the very place of our failure to restore us?
Peter's restoration flowed straight into a commission to feed the sheep. How might Christ be turning your own failures into a deeper, humbler usefulness?
Jesus told Peter to stop comparing his path to John's and simply follow. Where does comparison distract you from your own call to follow Christ?
The Gospel ends with follow Me. As you finish this study of John, what is the next step of following Jesus that He is placing before you?

translationNotes.note: The Sea of Tiberias is another name for the Sea of Galilee, as the BSB footnote notes; the disciples had returned to their home region.

Jesus' questions use agapao, and Peter answers with phileo; interpreters differ on whether the change is a meaningful distinction or stylistic variation common in John. The clear point is Peter's full threefold restoration.
Stretch out your hands and be led where you do not want to go indicates, John explains, the kind of death by which Peter would glorify God, understood as martyrdom.
The narrator corrects a false rumor that the beloved disciple would not die; Jesus posed only a hypothetical, a caution against building beliefs on misunderstood sayings.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 21:1-25. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped, embedded speaker turns joined with single spaces. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
